title: Advanced String Methods
subtitle: Join, split, strip, check, and align your strings like a pro
learning_objectives: Use .join() to combine a list of strings into one
Use .split() to break a string apart by a separator
Convert strings to title case with .title()

sections.heading: Joining and Splitting Strings
sections.content:
Two of the most useful string methods are .join() and .split(). They are opposites of each other.

.join(list) is called on a separator string and takes a list of strings, gluing them together with that separator between each item.

.split(sep) is called on a string and splits it into a list wherever the separator appears. If you call it with no argument it splits on any whitespace.
sections.code_example:
words = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
result = ", ".join(words)
print(result)

sentence = "one,two,three"
parts = sentence.split(",")
print(parts)

phrase = "hello world foo"
print(phrase.split())

sections.heading: Stripping Whitespace and Checking Contents
sections.content:
.strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) from a string. .lstrip() removes only from the left side and .rstrip() only from the right side. These are extremely handy when processing user input or file data.

Python also has boolean methods that inspect what a string contains:
  • .isdigit() — True if every character is a digit
  • .isalpha() — True if every character is a letter
  • .isalnum() — True if every character is a letter or digit (no spaces or symbols)
sections.code_example:
raw = "   hello   "
print(raw.strip())
print(raw.lstrip())
print(raw.rstrip())

print("12345".isdigit())
print("hello".isalpha())
print("abc123".isalnum())
print("abc 123".isalnum())
sections.code_output:
hello
hello   
   hello
True
True
True
False
sections.note: .strip() does NOT modify the original string — it returns a new one. Strings are immutable in Python.

sections.heading: Alignment and Title Case
sections.content:
Python lets you pad strings to a fixed width for neat output. All three methods take a width argument and optionally a fill character (default is a space):
  • .center(width) — centres the text
  • .ljust(width) — left-justifies (pads on the right)
  • .rjust(width) — right-justifies (pads on the left)

.title() capitalises the first letter of every word, which is great for formatting names or headings.
sections.code_example:
name = "Python"
print(name.center(20))
print(name.ljust(20, "-"))
print(name.rjust(20, "."))

title = "the quick brown fox"
print(title.title())
sections.code_output:
Python       
Python--------------
..............Python
The Quick Brown Fox

exercises.title: Build a CSV Line
exercises.prompt: You have a list called fruits containing "mango", "kiwi", and "grape". Use .join() to create a single comma-separated string called csv_line and print it. The output should be: mango,kiwi,grape
exercises.starter_code:
fruits = ["mango", "kiwi", "grape"]
# Create a variable called csv_line by joining fruits with a comma separator
# Print csv_line
hints: Call .join() on the separator string, not on the list. Example: "SEP".join(my_list)
The separator here is a comma with no spaces: ","

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.

exercises.title: Clean and Validate Input
exercises.prompt: The variable user_input is set to " 42 ". Strip the whitespace, then check whether the stripped value is all digits using .isdigit(). Print the stripped value and then print the result of the digit check on separate lines.
exercises.starter_code:
user_input = "  42  "
# Create a variable called cleaned by stripping whitespace from user_input
# Print the cleaned value
# Check if cleaned is all digits and print the result
hints: Use user_input.strip() to remove whitespace from both sides.
Then call .isdigit() on the cleaned string.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
The stripped value '42' should be printed.
'True' should be printed because '42' is all digits.

exercises.title: Format a Name Tag
exercises.prompt: The variable name is set to "alice smith". Convert it to title case and then print it centred in a field of width 30 using dashes (-) as the fill character.
exercises.starter_code:
name = "alice smith"
# Create a variable called titled by converting name to title case
# Print titled centred in a field of width 30 using '-' as fill

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
The name should be in title case.

quiz.question: What does "-".join(["a", "b", "c"]) return?
quiz.explanation: .join() places the separator string between each list item, so "-".join(["a","b","c"]) gives "a-b-c".

quiz.question: Which method returns True only when every character in the string is a letter (no digits or spaces)?
quiz.explanation: .isalpha() returns True only when every character is an alphabetic letter. .isalnum() allows digits too, and .isdigit() requires all digits.

completion_message: Excellent work! You now have a solid toolkit of advanced string methods. These come up constantly in real Python programs whenever you process text, clean data, or format output.
